section.title: The Oracle against Moab
section.sectionRationale: The third and longest of the smaller oracles against the nations turns to Moab, Israel's kin through Lot and a long-time rival across the Jordan. Town after Moabite town is named and mourned, Moab's pride and trust in Chemosh and in her own wealth are exposed, and even the LORD Himself is pictured weeping over her, before the oracle ends with an unexpected promise of restoration in the latter days.
section.thesis: Moab is broken for trusting her own works, wealth, and god Chemosh instead of the LORD; yet the same LORD who shatters her like an unwanted jar weeps for her fall and promises to restore her in the latter days.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: woe to Moab's proud cities; trust in wealth and Chemosh ends in exile; the scepter shattered, mourning across every town; ridicule for the proud, drunk on judgment; the LORD Himself weeps for Moab; universal mourning, shattered like an unwanted jar; and the eagle, the pit, and the snare, yet mercy in the latter days.

verses.text: Concerning Moab, this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: “Woe to Nebo, for it will be devastated. Kiriathaim will be captured and disgraced; the fortress will be shattered and dismantled.
There is no longer praise for Moab; in Heshbon they devise evil against her: ‘Come, let us cut her off from nationhood.’ You too, O people of Madmen, will be silenced; the sword will pursue you.
A voice cries out from Horonaim: ‘Devastation and great destruction!’
Moab will be shattered; her little ones will cry out.
For on the ascent to Luhith they weep bitterly as they go, and on the descent to Horonaim cries of distress resound over the destruction:
‘Flee! Run for your lives! Become like a juniper in the desert.’

units.label: Trust in wealth and Chemosh ends in exile
units.note: settled on the dregs, soon poured out

verses.text: Because you trust in your works and treasures, you too will be captured, and {{chemosh|Chemosh}} will go into exile with his priests and officials.
The destroyer will move against every city, and not one town will escape. The valley will also be ruined, and the high plain will be destroyed, as the LORD has said.
Put salt on Moab, for she will be laid waste; her cities will become desolate, with no one to dwell in them.
Cursed is the one who is remiss in doing the work of the LORD, and cursed is he who withholds his sword from bloodshed.
Moab has been at ease from youth, settled like wine on its dregs; he has not been poured from vessel to vessel or gone into exile. So his flavor has remained the same, and his aroma is unchanged.
Therefore behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will send to him wanderers, who will pour him out. They will empty his vessels and shatter his jars.
Then Moab will be ashamed of Chemosh, just as the house of Israel was ashamed when they trusted in Bethel.

units.label: The scepter shattered, mourning across every town
units.note: boasting brought low, a roll call of ruins

verses.text: How can you say, ‘We are warriors, mighty men ready for battle’?
Moab has been destroyed and its towns have been invaded; the best of its young men have gone down in the slaughter, declares the King, whose name is the LORD of Hosts.
Moab’s calamity is at hand, and his affliction is rushing swiftly.
Mourn for him, all you who surround him, everyone who knows his name; tell how the mighty scepter is shattered, the glorious staff!
Come down from your glory; sit on parched ground, O daughter dwelling in Dibon, for the destroyer of Moab has come against you; he has destroyed your fortresses.
Stand by the road and watch, O dweller of Aroer! Ask the man fleeing or the woman escaping, ‘What has happened?’
Moab is put to shame, for it has been shattered. Wail and cry out! Declare by the Arnon that Moab is destroyed.
Judgment has come upon the high plain: upon Holon, Jahzah, and Mephaath,
upon Dibon, Nebo, and Beth-diblathaim,
upon Kiriathaim, Beth-gamul, and Beth-meon,
upon Kerioth, Bozrah, and all the towns of Moab, those far and near.
The horn of Moab has been cut off, and his arm is broken,” declares the LORD.

units.label: Ridicule for the proud, drunk on judgment

verses.text: “Make him drunk, because he has magnified himself against the LORD; so Moab will wallow in his own vomit, and he will also become a laughingstock.
Was not Israel your object of ridicule? Was he ever found among thieves? For whenever you speak of him you shake your head.
Abandon the towns and settle among the rocks, O dwellers of Moab! Be like a dove that nests at the mouth of a cave.

units.label: The LORD Himself weeps for Moab

verses.text: We have heard of Moab’s {{gaon|pomposity}}, his exceeding pride and conceit, his proud arrogance and haughtiness of heart.
I know his insolence,” declares the LORD, “but it is futile. His boasting is as empty as his deeds.
Therefore I will wail for Moab; I will cry out for all of Moab; I will moan for the men of Kir-heres.

I will weep for you, O vine of Sibmah, more than I weep for Jazer. Your tendrils have extended to the sea; they reach even to Jazer. The destroyer has descended on your summer fruit and grape harvest.
Joy and gladness are removed from the orchard and from the fields of Moab. I have stopped the flow of wine from the presses; no one treads them with shouts of joy; their shouts are not for joy.
There is a cry from Heshbon to Elealeh; they raise their voices to Jahaz, from Zoar to Horonaim and Eglath-shelishiyah; for even the waters of Nimrim have dried up.
In Moab, declares the LORD, I will bring an end to those who make offerings on the high places and burn incense to their gods.
Therefore My heart laments like a flute for Moab; it laments like a flute for the men of Kir-heres, because the wealth they acquired has perished.

units.label: Universal mourning, shattered like an unwanted jar

verses.text: For every head is shaved and every beard is clipped; on every hand is a gash, and around every waist is sackcloth.
On all the rooftops of Moab and in the public squares, everyone is mourning; for I have shattered Moab like an unwanted jar,” declares the LORD.
“How shattered it is! How they wail! How Moab has turned his back in shame! Moab has become an object of ridicule and horror to all those around him.”

units.label: The eagle, the pit, and the snare, yet mercy in the latter days

verses.text: For this is what the LORD says: “Behold, an eagle swoops down and spreads his wings against Moab.
Kirioth has been taken, and the strongholds seized. In that day the heart of Moab’s warriors will be like the heart of a woman in labor.
Moab will be destroyed as a nation because he vaunted himself against the LORD.
Terror and pit and snare await you, O dweller of Moab,” declares the LORD.
“Whoever flees the panic will fall into the pit, and whoever climbs from the pit will be caught in the snare. For I will bring upon Moab the year of their punishment,” declares the LORD.
“Those who flee will stand helpless in Heshbon’s shadow, because fire has gone forth from Heshbon and a flame from within Sihon. It devours the foreheads of Moab and the skulls of the sons of tumult.
Woe to you, O Moab! The people of Chemosh have perished; for your sons have been taken into exile and your daughters have gone into captivity.
Yet in the latter days I will restore Moab from captivity,” declares the LORD. Here ends the judgment on Moab.

chemosh.gloss: Chemosh, the national god of Moab, to whom Moabite kings offered sacrifice, even child sacrifice in extremity, 2 Kings 3:27. That Chemosh goes into exile with his own priests, verse 7, shows he cannot save the people who trusted him.

gaon.gloss: Pride, pomposity, or exaltation, here describing Moab's proud arrogance and haughtiness of heart, verse 29. The same root can describe legitimate majesty, but turned toward self, it becomes the very thing the LORD opposes and brings down.

basic.context: This is the longest of the shorter oracles against the nations, aimed at Moab, the kingdom east of the Dead Sea descended from Lot, Israel's kin and frequent rival. Town after Moabite town, Nebo, Kiriathaim, Heshbon, Dibon, Kerioth, and more, is named and mourned in turn. Moab's sin is trust in her own works, wealth, and long ease, and in Chemosh her god, none of which can save her when the destroyer comes; yet astonishingly, the LORD Himself is pictured weeping over Moab's fall, and the oracle ends with a promise of restoration.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes an actual historical judgment on Moab, largely fulfilled through Babylonian campaigns in the early sixth century BC that ended Moab's existence as a distinct kingdom. It is not a coded message about any nation today. The lasting truth is that ease and self-sufficiency, settling like wine undisturbed on its dregs, make a people brittle rather than strong, and that no idol can rescue those who trust it.
basic.originalLanguages: Chemosh, Moab's national god, is named three times, always to show his helplessness: he goes into exile with his own priests, verse 7, and his people perish with him, verse 46. Moab's core sin is named with the word ga'own, pride or pomposity, verse 29, the same haughtiness of heart that runs through every oracle against the nations in this section.
basic.typology: Moab shattered like an unwanted jar, verse 38, pictures the fate of every vessel of wrath prepared for destruction, Romans 9:22, in contrast to vessels of mercy prepared beforehand for glory. The LORD's own weeping over Moab, verses 31 to 36, anticipates Christ weeping over Jerusalem, Luke 19:41, the same divine grief poured out even upon those under righteous judgment.
basic.christ: Remarkably, the LORD does not merely announce Moab's judgment; He weeps for it, wailing and moaning like a flute in lament, verses 31 to 36. That grief over necessary judgment finds its clearest echo in Christ, who wept over Jerusalem even while pronouncing the very judgment that was coming upon her, Luke 19:41 to 44. And where Moab's pride and trust in Chemosh brought her down, Christ is the one truly exalted King who humbled Himself and was therefore lifted up, Philippians 2:8-9, the opposite pattern to Moab's proud fall.
basic.god: God opposes pride absolutely: Moab's ga'own, her proud arrogance, is named as the very root of her downfall, verse 29. Yet God's justice is never cold; He grieves even as He judges, and He does not close the book on Moab forever, promising restoration in the latter days, verse 47, the same shape of mercy folded into judgment found throughout these oracles.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 21:29 and Isaiah 15 to 16 give earlier oracles against Moab. 2 Kings 3:27 records Moab's king offering his own son to Chemosh in desperation. Luke 19:41-44 shows Christ weeping over Jerusalem's coming judgment. Romans 9:22 speaks of vessels of wrath prepared for destruction, echoing the image of Moab shattered like a jar.
basic.oneStory: Moab's proud self-sufficiency and trust in Chemosh could not save her from the LORD's justice, yet the same LORD who judged her wept over her fall and promised restoration in the latter days. That pattern, judgment held together with grief and hope, comes to its fullest expression in Christ, who wept over Jerusalem even as He went to bear judgment Himself, so that mercy might reach every proud nation humbled before God.

context.literary: The third and longest panel among the smaller oracles against the nations, built as a catalogue lament, naming town after Moabite town, and shifting unexpectedly into divine mourning, verses 31 to 36, before closing with a promise of restoration, verse 47, a pattern shared with the endings of the oracles on Ammon and Elam.
context.historical: Moab occupied the plateau east of the Dead Sea and the Arnon river, a kingdom often at war with Israel and Judah, subjugated by Babylon in the early sixth century BC; archaeological and textual evidence shows Moab effectively disappeared as a distinct people not long after, a fulfillment of this oracle's severity.
context.canonical: Moab is judged repeatedly across the prophets, Isaiah 15 to 16, Amos 2:1-3, Zephaniah 2:8-11, and Ezekiel 25:8-11, and yet a Moabite woman, Ruth, becomes an ancestor of David and of Christ, a striking reminder that the LORD's mercy reaches across even the harshest of these national judgments.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce Moab's coming devastation for her pride, her trust in Chemosh, and her long, untroubled ease, while showing that the LORD who judges nations also grieves their fall and holds out hope of future restoration.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment on Moab; it does not prescribe an attitude of contempt toward Moabites or any people today. The abiding principle is that untested ease breeds pride, and pride invites the very judgment it least expects.

debates.question: Why does the LORD Himself weep over a nation He is actively judging, verses 31 to 36?

views.label: Judgment and compassion coexist in God's character
views.case: Scripture consistently presents the LORD as both just and merciful at once; He takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked, Ezekiel 33:11, so grieving over Moab's fall while still bringing it about reflects His full character, not a contradiction.

views.label: The lament voices what Moab herself should feel but cannot
views.case: Some read the weeping as prophetic identification, Jeremiah speaking in the LORD's name to voice the grief Moab's own proud heart refuses to feel over her sin, modeling true repentance she never offers.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage refuses to let judgment become a triumphant celebration; even righteous justice is carried out with sorrow, not delight, foreshadowing Christ's own tears over Jerusalem.

study.originalLanguages: Chemosh, Moab's god, is named three times, each occurrence exposing his powerlessness, verses 7, 13, and 46, as he goes into exile with his own worshipers. The word ga'own, pride or pomposity, verse 29, names the root sin under every ruin catalogued in this chapter, the same haughtiness of heart the LORD opposes throughout Scripture.

typology.type: Moab shattered like an unwanted jar
typology.fulfillment: The vessels of wrath prepared for destruction, set against the vessels of mercy prepared for glory, Romans 9:22-23.

typology.type: The LORD weeping over Moab's judgment
typology.fulfillment: Christ weeping over Jerusalem even as He pronounced her coming judgment, Luke 19:41-44.

study.christ: Remarkably, the LORD does not merely announce Moab's judgment; He weeps for it, wailing and moaning like a flute in lament, verses 31 to 36. That grief over necessary judgment finds its clearest echo in Christ, who wept over Jerusalem even while pronouncing the very judgment that was coming upon her, Luke 19:41 to 44. And where Moab's pride and trust in Chemosh brought her down, Christ is the one truly exalted King who humbled Himself and was therefore lifted up, Philippians 2:8-9, the opposite pattern to Moab's proud fall.
study.god: God opposes pride absolutely: Moab's ga'own, her proud arrogance, is named as the very root of her downfall, verse 29. Yet God's justice is never cold; He grieves even as He judges, and He does not close the book on Moab forever, promising restoration in the latter days, verse 47, the same shape of mercy folded into judgment found throughout these oracles.

crossReferences.note: An earlier, similarly grief-laden oracle against Moab.
Records the king of Moab sacrificing his own son to Chemosh in desperation, showing the emptiness of Moab's trust.
Christ weeps over Jerusalem's coming judgment, the same pattern of grief joined to justice.
Vessels of wrath prepared for destruction, echoing the image of Moab shattered like a jar, verse 38.

study.oneStory: Moab's proud self-sufficiency and trust in Chemosh could not save her from the LORD's justice, yet the same LORD who judged her wept over her fall and promised restoration in the latter days. That pattern, judgment held together with grief and hope, comes to its fullest expression in Christ, who wept over Jerusalem even as He went to bear judgment Himself, so that mercy might reach every proud nation humbled before God.

quiz.q: According to verse 7, what does Moab trust in that will not save her?

options.text: Her own works and treasures, alongside her god Chemosh.
options.note: Verse 7 names this exact combination of self-reliance and false worship as the reason for Moab's capture.

options.text: Her alliance with Egypt.
options.note: No Egyptian alliance is mentioned in this oracle; the failure named is trust in works, treasure, and Chemosh.

options.text: Her army's superior weapons.
options.note: Moab's warriors are addressed later in the chapter, but the specific trust named in verse 7 is her works, treasures, and Chemosh.

options.text: Her covenant with the LORD.
options.note: Moab was never in covenant with the LORD; her trust was in her own works and in Chemosh.

quiz.q: What image describes Moab's long, untroubled ease in verse 11?

options.text: Wine settled undisturbed on its dregs, never poured from vessel to vessel.
options.note: Verse 11 uses exactly this image to describe Moab's untested comfort.

options.text: A tree planted by flowing streams.
options.note: This is a positive image used elsewhere in Scripture, not the image used here.

options.text: A city built on solid rock.
options.note: Not the image in this passage; Moab's stability is pictured as false ease, not sound foundation.

options.text: A field left fallow for planting.
options.note: Related in spirit to untouched stillness, but the specific image given is wine settled on its dregs.

quiz.q: How does the LORD respond to Moab's coming judgment in verses 31 to 36?

options.text: He weeps and laments for Moab like a mourning flute, even while pronouncing her judgment.
options.note: These verses show the LORD Himself grieving Moab's fall, a striking picture of justice joined with sorrow.

options.text: He rejoices openly over her downfall.
options.note: The opposite is shown; the LORD mourns rather than celebrates Moab's ruin.

options.text: He remains entirely silent about Moab's fate.
options.note: The LORD speaks extensively and personally throughout this chapter.

options.text: He blames Israel for provoking Moab's judgment.
options.note: Not the content of these verses; the LORD's grief here is directed at Moab's own fall, not at Israel's role.

quiz.q: What hope closes the oracle against Moab in verse 47?

options.text: The LORD promises to restore Moab from captivity in the latter days.
options.note: Verse 47 explicitly holds out this hope even after such thorough judgment.

options.text: Moab is promised she will never again face any judgment.
options.note: Overstated; judgment on Moab is very real, but restoration is promised for a future day.

options.text: Moab will conquer all her former enemies.
options.note: No such promise appears; the hope given is restoration, not conquest.

options.text: There is no hope offered for Moab at all.
options.note: Verse 47 explicitly offers hope of restoration in the latter days.

quiz.q: How does the LORD's weeping over Moab anticipate Christ?

options.text: It foreshadows Christ weeping over Jerusalem even as He pronounced her coming judgment.
options.note: Luke 19:41-44 shows Christ grieving over the very judgment He announces, echoing this same pattern.

options.text: It shows that God never actually judges anyone.
options.note: The chapter is full of real, severe judgment on Moab; grief does not cancel justice.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Scripture draws a clear parallel between this divine grief and Christ's tears over Jerusalem.

options.text: It suggests judgment is always a mistake to be regretted.
options.note: The grief shown is sorrow over necessary judgment, not regret that it was wrong to bring it.

questions: Moab had settled like wine undisturbed on its dregs, never tested by hardship. Where has comfort made you complacent rather than dependent on God?
Moab's pride, her ga'own, is named as the root of her fall. Where does pride quietly shape your own decisions or self-image?
Chemosh could not save the people who trusted him, and went into exile with them. What are you trusting that, in the end, cannot actually deliver you?
The LORD wept even as He judged Moab. How does it change your view of God's justice to know that He takes no delight in judgment, even when it is deserved?
Even after such thorough judgment, the LORD promised to restore Moab in the latter days. Where do you need to believe that God's mercy can still reach a situation you have written off as beyond hope?

translationNotes.note: Poetic line breaks in these verses have been joined into running verse text per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash, it has been rendered as a comma or colon per house style.
A stray space before closing punctuation in the source text has been corrected.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Jeremiah 48:1-47. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; em dashes rendered as commas or colons per house style.
